# Luke 16: The Deceit of Divided Hearts

Jesus doesn't teach here by condemning fraud outright—He teaches by showing you what you already are. The dishonest manager, facing ruin, does the only thing his heart knows how to do: he schemes to secure his future. His master actually commends his shrewdness, not his dishonesty. The jarring lesson lands hard: the manager will face consequences for his actions, but what Jesus wants you to see is that he's simply being faithful to his true master—money. He's shrewd precisely because money is his god, and he serves it with single-minded devotion. Then Jesus turns this logic directly at you. If you claim to love God but live for wealth, you're not being inconsistent—you're being perfectly consistent with your actual master. The language is absolute: "No servant can serve two masters." This isn't a gentle suggestion about balance or moderation. You cannot divide your allegiance. Your heart will inevitably orient itself toward one lord and turn against the other. The Pharisees listening understood exactly what He meant, which is why they scoffed. Jesus answers by exposing the real rot: they justify themselves before people, but God knows hearts. What men prize—wealth, status, the appearance of righteousness—God calls detestable.

The parable of Lazarus and the rich man then shows you where this divided loyalty leads. The rich man isn't described as actively wicked; he simply lives in joyous splendor while Lazarus, visible at his very gate, starves. The rich man's sin is indifference. He had the means to help and chose not to. When he dies and faces torment in Hades, he suddenly becomes aware of justice—but awareness comes too late. He begs Abraham to send Lazarus to cool his tongue, then to warn his living brothers. Notice his two requests: both put the burden on someone else. He doesn't repent; he asks for rescue. Abraham's response is devastating: "They have Moses and the Prophets; let your brothers listen to them." The rich man protests—surely a resurrection would convince them—but Abraham is unmoved. "If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead." This is a hard truth about yourself: you already have what you need to know God's will. You have Scripture. You have the gospel. The barrier to obedience isn't lack of knowledge or lack of a sign. It's your heart.

What Luke 16 forces you to confront is that your choices about money, your indifference to the poor, your divided loyalties—these aren't small failures of discipline. They reveal your actual master. You cannot claim allegiance to God while your wealth dictates your security, your decisions, and your priorities. The parable doesn't promise that repentance is impossible—Jesus is speaking to the living, after all—but it makes unmistakably clear that the only currency that matters in eternity is faithfulness with what God has given you now. If you cannot be trusted with temporary earthly wealth, why would God entrust you with anything lasting? The question isn't whether you need to be more responsible with money. It's whether God is actually your master, or whether you're like the dishonest manager—serving with perfect consistency the only god you really believe in.
